revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:百済三書]][[ko:백제삼서]][[zh:百濟三書]]


'''Kudara Sansho''' (百済三書, "Three Books of Baekje") refers to the collective name for the ''{{ill|Baekje Chronicle|ja|百済記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Baekje Chronicle|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 百済三書|1=Baekje Annals|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11244191}}'' (Kudara-ki), the ''{{ill|Baekje 11 Compilation|ja|百済新撰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Baekje 11 Compilation|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 百済三書|1=Baekje Annals|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11244191}}'' (Kudara Shinpen), and the ''{{ill|Baekje Primary Records|ja|百済本記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Baekje Primary Records|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 百済三書|1=Baekje Annals|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11244191}}'' (Kudara Honki). These three texts recorded the history of [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]], an ancient Korean kingdom. None of the original texts have survived, but fragments (known as {{ill|Lost literary work|en|Lost literary work|qid=Q1585442|lt=Itubun|1=Lost literary work}}) are quoted in the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|qid=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}''.
It is important to note that ''Baekje Primary Records'' (百済本記) in this context differs from the "Baekje Bon'gi" included in the ''{{ill|Samguk sagi|en|Samguk sagi|qid=Q485552|lt=Samguk Sagi|1=Samguk sagi}}.''
== Contents ==
From the fragments quoted in the ''Nihon Shoki,'' it is evident that the three books covered nearly 200 years of Baekje history, spanning from {{ill|Geunchogo of Baekje|en|Geunchogo of Baekje|qid=Q497601|lt=Geunchogo of Baekje|1=Geunchogo of Baekje}} to {{ill|Wideok of Baekje|ja|威徳王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wideok of Baekje|1=Wideok of Baekje|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1208848}}. ''Baekje Chronicle'' (Kudara-ki), the oldest among the three, contains narrative-style descriptions, while ''Baekje 11 Compilation'' (Kudara Shinpen) is written in a chronicle-like style, and ''Baekje Primary Records'' (Kudara Honki) employs a purely annalistic format. {{ill|Inoue Mitsusada|ja|井上光貞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inoue Mitsusada|1=Mitsusada Inoue|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11372857}} has hypothesized these differences in style.{{Sfn|日本古典文学大系『日本書紀 上』|1967|巻第九補注37|qq=}}
The books also include numerous names, some of which the ''Nihon Shoki'' editors equated with Japanese figures. For instance, ''職麻那加比跪'' (Shikumanakahiko) is identified with {{ill|Chikuma Nagahiko|ja|千熊長彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikuma Nagahiko|1=Chikuma Nagahiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q22130189}}, and ''沙至比跪'' (Sachihiko) with {{ill|Katsuragi Sotsuhiko|ja|葛城襲津彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsuragi Sotsuhiko|1=Katsuragi Sotsuhiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11620922}}.{{efn|''Shikumanakahiko'' is explicitly identified with Chikuma Nagahiko in a footnote of the ''Nihon Shoki.'' ''Sachihiko'' appears in Katsuragi Sotsuhiko's biography.|qq=}}
== Composition ==
The precise process of how these three books were compiled is unknown, as they are only preserved as fragments in the ''Nihon Shoki.''
{{ill|Imanishi Ryu|ja|今西竜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imanishi Ryu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} has suggested that Baekje began recording written histories during the reign of its 13th ruler, {{ill|Geunchogo of Baekje|en|Geunchogo of Baekje|qid=Q497601|lt=Geunchogo of Baekje|1=Geunchogo of Baekje}}, based on the ''Samguk Sagi,'' which notes that Baekje started keeping written records in 375 CE under the guidance of the scholar {{ill|Go Heung|ja|高興|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Go Heung|1=Go Heung|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q15891272}}.{{Sfn|Imanishi|1970|p=79|qq=}} 
{{ill|Shōei Mishina|en|Shōei Mishina|qid=Q11354687|lt=Mishina Akihide|1=Shōei Mishina}} argued that the titles of the kings in ''Baekje Chronicle,'' such as "肖古王" (Shōko-Ō) and "貴須王" (Kisu-Ō), differ from later fabrications in other records, suggesting that the ''Nihon Shoki'' genealogy may be more reliable.{{Sfn|Mishina|1962|pp=103–105|qq=}}
Imanishi further theorized that the ''Baekje Primary Records'' were written by Baekje scholars around 620 CE to present to the Japanese court. Mishina placed the creation of ''Baekje Chronicle'' earlier, in the late 6th or early 7th century.{{Sfn|Imanishi|1970|pp=79–80|qq=}}
Inoue Mitsusada suggested that the transmission of records from Baekje to Japan occurred during the kingdom's downfall in 660 CE, when many Baekje refugees fled to Japan. These records might have been incorporated into the three texts by Japanese scholars between 663 CE and 720 CE.{{Sfn|井上光貞『日本の歴史1 神話から歴史へ』|1973|qq=}}
{{ill|Endo Keita|ja|遠藤慶太|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Endo Keita|1=Keita Endō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q21699226}} proposed that the ''Baekje Chronicle'' and ''Baekje Primary Records'' were composed in the early 7th century by Baekje émigrés in Japan, emphasizing their lineage and historical ties with Japan as a political appeal against {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|qid=Q28456|lt=Silla|1=Silla}}.{{Sfn|遠藤慶太『日本書紀の形成と諸資料』|2015|qq=}}
== Quotations in the ''Nihon Shoki'' ==
The ''Nihon Shoki'' explicitly cites ''Baekje Chronicle'' five times, ''Baekje 11 Compilation'' three times, and ''Baekje Primary Records'' 18 times. Some of these quotations use terms like "Emperor" (天皇) or "Japan" (日本), which became standard only in the 7th century, suggesting later embellishments by the ''Nihon Shoki'' editors.{{Sfn|津田左右吉|qq=}}
However, there are instances where the ''Nihon Shoki'' adheres to the original records, such as in dating the death of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|en|Emperor Keitai|qid=Q349277|lt=Emperor Keitai|1=Emperor Keitai}}. This consistency suggests that not all entries in the three books were fabricated by the ''Nihon Shoki'' editors.{{Sfn|井上光貞|2007|qq=}}
== Notes ==

* {{Cite book|和書|last=今西|first=竜|title=百済史研究|publisher=国書刊行会|date=1970|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/12173008|doi=10.11501/12173008|ref=harv|qq=}}
